# Introduction of X-ray diffraction

## Definition
In material researh, the scientist and materials researchers have many analytical question related to the chemical composition and crystalline constitution of materials. These question can be answered by X-ray diffraction measurement. 

:::{.callout-note appearance="minimal"} 
## Definition
X-ray diffraction or X-ray diffractometer (XRD) is a non-destructively analytical technique used to analyze physical properties, primarily at the atomic or molecule level, such as phase composition, crystal structure, crystallite size, lattice strain, preferred orientation, layer thickness and orientation of powder, solid, and liquids samples. 
:::

## Purpose
The main purpose of XRD is to validate whether the syntesized crystal results are consistent with the agreement in theoritical model or previously reported works in the literature.

## Pre-requisite understanding
Before we start to interpret XRD results, there are some questions on the basics of XRD machines and crystallography structure. Check the box for each questions you already answer.

### 1. Basics of XRD techniques

>   - [ ] How is the working mechanisme of XRD machines?
>   - [ ] What types of measurement are typically made?
>   - [ ] What is Bragg's Law equation?
>   - [ ] What exactly is the Bragg angle and how is it measured in XRD characterisation?

### 2. Crystallographic terminologies

>  - [ ] What is the difference between a crystalline and amorphous materials?
>  - [ ] What are the seven crystal systems?
>  - [ ] How many point symmetry groups (crystal classes) actually?

### 3. Lattice and Unit cell

>  - [ ] What are crystal lattice and unit cell ?
>  - [ ] How many Bravais lattice that consist in crystal systems?
>  - [ ] What is polycrystalline materials, and how is it different from single crystal?

### 4. Lattice planes and Directions

>  - [ ] What are Miller indiches?
>  - [ ] What is the difference between a lattice plane (hkl) and lattice direction (uvw)?

If you are not ready to answer confidently, don't worry!. We can learn from these videos and references below, before continuing the next steps.
